response:
## IA drift verdicts

**`crime_00020` — route_metadata_drift (`/settings/billing`) → warn**

This is the headline finding and the most useful of the three. One destination has five competing identities:

| Surface | Name |
|---|---|
| URL path | `billing` |
| file | `billing.tsx` |
| component | `PricingPage` |
| `metadata.title` | Plans |
| `<title>` | Subscription |
| nav (registry) | Plans |
| nav (sidebar) | Billing |

A user clicking "Billing" lands on a tab titled "Subscription" rendered by `PricingPage`. It still loads, so block-merge is overkill — but an agent asked to "rename the Subscription page" has five plausible places to start and will leave four of them stale. Worth fixing before merge; the human action is "pick one canonical name and align the surfaces."

**`crime_00018` — duplicated_navigation_source (`/settings/billing`) → warn (treat as part of #00020)**

This is the root cause of two of #00020's seven evidence lines — `registry.ts` labels the link "Plans" while `sidebar.ts` calls it "Billing". Don't triage it separately; pin it as a related finding under #00020 and the fix (consolidate to one nav source of truth, or document which one is canonical) closes both. Don't double-count the risk.

**`crime_00022` — docs_code_drift (`docs/teams.md → ./setup.md`) → warn**

Low severity, single broken link, but **confidence 0.9** and the agent_risk justification is exactly right: an agent reading `docs/teams.md` will follow `./setup.md` and silently get nothing. Cheap to fix — either restore the file or strike the link. Only ignore if `docs/teams.md` is itself known-stale (which the `concept_alias_drift` finding hints at — it's already named in #00015 as part of the `tenant`-alias mess in the docs). If the doc is on the chopping block, deleting it resolves this anyway.

### Net recommendation

All three are **warn, not block** — IA is misaligned, not broken — but #00020 + #00018 should be treated as a single must-fix unit (pick the canonical name for that destination and align everything), and #00022 is a one-line cleanup that's worth doing now because it's almost free.
